en.extract:
The greater painted-snipe (Rostratula benghalensis) is a species of wader in the small painted-snipe family Rostratulidae. It widely distributed across Africa and southern Asia and is found in a variety of wetland habitats, including swamps and the edges of larger water bodies such as lakes and rivers. This species is sexually dimorphic with the female being larger and more brightly coloured than the male. The female is normally polyandrous with the males incubating the eggs and caring for the young.


Taxonomy
The greater painted-snipe was formally described in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in the tenth edition of his Systema Naturae. He placed it with the rails in the genus Rallus and coined the binomial name Rallus benghalenis. Linnaeus based his account on the "Bengall water rail" that had been described and illustrated in 1738 by the English naturalist Eleazar Albin in his A Natural History of Birds. Albin had examined a drawing that had been sent to the English silk-pattern designer Joseph Dandridge from Bengal. The greater painted-snipe is now placed with the Australian painted-snipe in the genus Rostratula that was introduced in 1816 by the French ornithologist Louis Vieillot. The species is treated as monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.
The Australian painted-snipe (Rostratula australis) was formerly treated as a subspecies but was promoted to species status based on the differences in morphology and in the vocal calls.


Description

The greater painted-snipe is a medium-sized shorebird with an overall length of 23–28 cm (9.1–11.0 in). The species is sexually dimorphic: females are larger, heavier, and have bolder plumage than males. The female has a black head with a buff stripe and a white eye-patch. The neck is dark rufous. The upperparts are mostly dark bronze-green finely barred with black. A white stripe curves around the shoulder mantle. The underbody is white. The male is much paler and less uniform with barring on the scapulars and wing-coverts. The juvenile resembles the male but lacks the darker band around the chest.
It is not a vocal species; apart from the breeding season, it is mostly silent. The female may make a "mellow hooting or booming" sound.


Distribution and habitat
Greater painted-snipe are very widely distributed; in mainland Africa as well as Madagascar and the Seychelles; in India, and Southeast Asia. Within Africa, they are found in the Nile River Valley and in the non-rainforested areas of Sub-Saharan Africa. They are notably absent from the eastern portion of Somalia, from the desert areas of Namibia, and from parts of Botswana and South Africa. Despite their wide distribution, they are uncommon within their range. There are between 31,000 and 1,000,000 mature individuals alive, according to BirdLife International.
Although this species inhabits a variety of wetland habitats, it prefers muddy areas with available cover (i.e., vegetation). It is also found on the edges of lakes and rivers, provided there is cover nearby, and in marshes and around swamps. They are usually found close to the fringes of reed beds along shorelines of marshes, swamps, ponds and streams.


Behaviour
Greater painted-snipe usually live solitarily or in pairs, but sometimes are found in large groups. They are rather shy and retiring, skulking close to the vegetation so that they can retreat to cover if disturbed. When flushed, the birds like rails, with their legs dangling.


Food and feeding
Feeds on insects, snails, earthworms and crustacea as well as vegetable matter such as plant seeds. Uses scythe-like action of the head and bill in shallow water. They are generally crepuscular, feeding in the early morning and near dusk.


Breeding
Greater painted-snipe are almost always polyandrous. The female initiates courtship and usually mates with two males in a season, but may mate with up to four. Males incubate the eggs and provide parental care. The females court the males, are polyandrous with males incubating and raising the young. The nest is a shallow scrape in soft ground, lined with plant material and situated among grass or reeds at the water's edge; sometimes a pad of vegetation or a nest of grass and weeds. It is usually well concealed. The clutch is normally 4 eggs. These have a light buff-yellow background and are covered with black-brown blotches, spots and lines. The eggs which measure 36 mm × 26 mm (1.4 in × 1.0 in) are incubated by the male for around 19 days. The young are precocial and nidifugous. They are brooded when they are small. The chicks are buff coloured and have black stripes running along their length.


Conservation status
The greater painted-snipe is as "Least Concern" by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN), due to its large range and the relatively slow rate of population decrease.

The '''greater painted-snipe''' ('''''Rostratula benghalensis''''') is a species of [[wader]] in the small painted-snipe family [[Rostratulidae]]. It widely distributed across Africa and southern Asia and is found in a variety of wetland habitats, including swamps and the edges of larger water bodies such as lakes and rivers. This species is sexually dimorphic with the female being larger and more brightly coloured than the male. The female is normally [[Polyandry in animals|polyandrous]] with the males incubating the eggs and caring for the young.

==Taxonomy==
The greater painted-snipe was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1758 by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in the [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|tenth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He placed it with the rails in the [[genus]] ''[[Rallus]]'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Rallus benghalenis''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1758 | title= Systema Naturae per regna tria naturae, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | volume=1 | edition=10th | page=153 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii | location=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=Latin | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/727060 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1934 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=2 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=230 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14483043 }}</ref> Linnaeus based his account on the "Bengall water rail" that had been described and illustrated in 1738 by the English naturalist [[Eleazar Albin]] in his ''A Natural History of Birds''. Albin had examined a drawing that had been sent to the English silk-pattern designer [[Joseph Dandridge]] from [[Bengal]].<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Albin | first1=Eleazar | author1-link=Eleazar Albin | last2=Derham | first2=William | author2-link=William Derham | year=1738 | title=A Natural History of Birds: Illustrated with a Hundred and One Copper Plates, Curiously Engraven from the Life | volume=3 | page=85, Plate 90 | location=London | publisher=Printed for the author and sold by William Innys | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41131169 }}</ref> The greater painted-snipe is now placed with the [[Australian painted-snipe]] in the genus ''[[Rostratula]]'' that was introduced in 1816 by the French ornithologist [[Louis Pierre Vieillot|Louis Vieillot]]. The species is treated as [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=December 2023 | title=Sandpipers, snipes, coursers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/sandpipers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=14 August 2024}}</ref>

The Australian painted-snipe (''Rostratula australis'') was formerly treated as a subspecies but was promoted to species status based on the differences in morphology and in the vocal calls.<ref name=ioc/><ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Lane | first1=B.A. | last2=Rogers | first2=D.I. | date=2000 | title=The Australian Painted Snipe, ''Rostratula'' (''benghalensis'') ''australis'': An endangered species? | journal=The Stilt | volume=36 | pages=26–34 | url=https://awsg.org.au/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/Stilt-36.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | last1=Christidis | first1=Les | last2=Boles | first2=Walter | year=2008 | title=Systematics and Taxonomy of Australian Birds | location=Collingwood, VIC, Australia | publisher=CSIRO | isbn=978-0-643-09602-8 | page=136 }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Painted Snipe hm.jpg|thumb|left|The female on the left is more colourful.]]
The greater painted-snipe is a medium-sized shorebird with an overall length of {{cvt|23|-|28|cm}}.<ref name=hbw/> The species is [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually dimorphic]]: females are larger, heavier, and have bolder plumage than males. The female has a black head with a buff stripe and a white eye-patch. The neck is dark rufous. The upperparts are mostly dark bronze-green finely barred with black. A white stripe curves around the shoulder mantle. The underbody is white. The male is much paler and less uniform with barring on the {{Birdgloss|scapulars}} and wing-coverts. The juvenile resembles the male but lacks the darker band around the chest.<ref name=bwp>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Cramp | editor1-first=Stanley | editor1-link=Stanley Cramp | year=1983 | chapter=''Rostratula benghalensis'' Painted snipe | title=Handbook of the Birds of Europe the Middle East and North Africa. The Birds of the Western Palearctic | volume=III: Waders to Gulls | location=Oxford | publisher=Oxford University Press | isbn=978-0-19-857506-1 | pages=10–16 }}</ref>

It is not a vocal species; apart from the breeding season, it is mostly silent. The female may make a "mellow hooting or booming" sound.<ref name=Elphick/>

== Distribution and habitat==
Greater painted-snipe are very widely distributed; in mainland Africa as well as Madagascar and the [[Seychelles]]; in India, and Southeast Asia.<ref name=Elphick>{{Cite book |last=Elphick |first=Jonathan |title=The World of Birds |publisher=Firefly Books |year=2014 |isbn=978-1-77085-304-1 |pages=360–361 |url=https://archive.org/details/worldofbirds0000elph/page/360/mode/1up | url-access=registration }}</ref><ref>{{ cite journal | last=Skerret | first=Adrian | date=2017 | title=First record of greater painted-snipe ''Rostratula benghalensis'' for Seychelles | journal=Bulletin of the African Bird Club | volume=24 | issue=1 | pages=92–93 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/60457556 }}</ref> Within Africa, they are found in the Nile River Valley and in the non-rainforested areas of [[Sub-Saharan Africa]]. They are notably absent from the eastern portion of Somalia, from the desert areas of Namibia, and from parts of Botswana and South Africa.<ref name=Navarro/> Despite their wide distribution, they are uncommon within their range.<ref name=Navarro/> There are between 31,000 and 1,000,000 mature individuals alive, according to [[BirdLife International]].<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=Greater Painted-snipe (Rostratula benghalensis) - BirdLife species factsheet |url=https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/greater-painted-snipe-rostratula-benghalensis |access-date=2023-12-23 |website=datazone.birdlife.org}}</ref>

Although this species inhabits a variety of wetland habitats, it prefers muddy areas with available cover (i.e., vegetation). It is also found on the edges of lakes and rivers, provided there is cover nearby, and in marshes and around swamps.<ref name=Navarro/> They are usually found close to the fringes of reed beds along shorelines of marshes, swamps, ponds and streams.

==Behaviour==
Greater painted-snipe usually live solitarily or in pairs, but sometimes are found in large groups. They are rather shy and retiring, skulking close to the vegetation so that they can retreat to cover if disturbed. When flushed, the birds like [[Rail (bird)|rails]], with their legs dangling.<ref name=Navarro>{{ cite book | last=Navarro | first=R.A. | chapter=Painted snipe | editor1-last=Harrison | editor1-first=J.A. | editor2-last=Allan | editor2-first=D.G. | editor3-last=Underhill | editor3-first=L.G. | editor4-last=Herremans | editor4-first=M. | editor5-last=Tree | editor5-first=A.J. | editor6-last=Parker | editor6-first=V. | editor7-last=Brown | editor7-first=C.J. | year=1997 | title=The Atlas of Southern African Birds | volume=1: Non-Passerines | location=Johannesburg  | publisher=BirdLife South Africa | pages=372–373 | isbn=978-0-620-20730-0 | chapter-url=https://sabap2.birdmap.africa/docs/sabap1/242.pdf }}</ref>

===Food and feeding===
Feeds on insects, snails, earthworms and crustacea as well as vegetable matter such as plant seeds. Uses scythe-like action of the head and bill in shallow water. They are generally crepuscular, feeding in the early morning and near dusk.<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last=Kirwan | first=G.M. | year=1996 | chapter=Family Rostratulidae (Painted-snipes) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=3: Hoatzin to Auks | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-87334-20-7 | pages=292-301 [300] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0003unse/page/292/mode/1up| chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref>

[[File:Rostratula benghalensis MHNT.jpg|thumb| upright=0.75|Egg, [[Muséum de Toulouse]]]]
[[File:Taijiang National Park Yi-Cheng, Chen 555594031-2.jpg|thumb|Male with chicks]]

===Breeding===
Greater painted-snipe are almost always polyandrous. The female initiates courtship and usually mates with two males in a season, but may mate with up to four. Males incubate the eggs and provide parental care.<ref name=Elphick/> The females court the males, are polyandrous<ref>{{cite journal|last=Komeda | first=Shigemoto |year=1983| title= Nest attendance of parent birds in the painted snipe (''Rostratula benghalensis'') | journal=The Auk|volume=100|issue=1|pages=48–55|doi=10.1093/auk/100.1.48 | doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{cite book| last=Wesley | first=H.D. | year=1993 | chapter= Breeding behaviour sequential polyandry and population decline in ''Rostratula benghalensis'' |title=Bird Conservation: Strategies for the Nineties and Beyond | editor1=Verghese, A |editor2=Sridhar, S |editor3=Chakravarthy, AK |publisher=Ornithological Society of India, Bangalore | pages=166–172| chapter-url=https://archive.org/stream/BirdConservationStrategies/BirdConservationIndia#page/n193/mode/1up}}</ref> with males incubating and raising the young. The nest is a shallow scrape in soft ground, lined with plant material and situated among grass or reeds at the water's edge; sometimes a pad of vegetation or a nest of grass and weeds. It is usually well concealed. The clutch is normally 4 eggs. These have a light buff-yellow background and are covered with black-brown blotches, spots and lines. The eggs which measure {{cvt|36|x|26|mm}} are incubated by the male for around 19 days. The young are [[precocial]] and [[nidifugous]]. They are brooded when they are small.<ref name=bwp/> The chicks are buff coloured and have black stripes running along their length.<ref>{{cite journal|title=A male painted snipe and his chick | last=Wesley | first=H. Daniel|journal=Newsletter for Birdwatchers | page=3 | volume=30 | issue=7&8 | url=https://archive.org/stream/NLBW31_78#page/n4/mode/1up }}</ref>

== Conservation status ==
The greater painted-snipe is as "Least Concern" by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] (IUCN), due to its large range and the relatively slow rate of population decrease.<ref name=iucn/>
